title: Zeros of Polynomial Functions
notes: Populate `dok2_anchors` and `dok3_anchors` with Savvas-declared examples from the textbook. The ingest script loads this file and sends it to Claude as reference BEFORE asking for a classification on a new screenshot. Keep 2-5 anchors per level. Prefer anchors whose rationale is easy to articulate in one sentence.

dok2_anchors.source: Savvas Practice #7 (Error Analysis, lesson 3-5, anchors Example 2 — multiplicity)
dok2_anchors.prompt: Tonya graphs f(x) = x³ + 3x² + 7x + 5 and concludes it has only one real root at x = −1. Describe and resolve two errors she made in finding all roots.
dok2_anchors.answer: Tonya neglected to use synthetic division to factor the polynomial after finding the real root, so she missed the complex roots −1 + 2i and −1 − 2i.
dok2_anchors.why_dok2: Apply a known multi-step procedure (find a real zero, divide, solve the quadratic) and notice what was skipped. Critique is shallow — the fix is procedural, not strategic. No novel reasoning required.

dok2_anchors.source: Savvas Practice #18 (lesson 3-5, anchors Example 4 — polynomial model)
dok2_anchors.prompt: Waterworks models paddleboard profit (in hundreds of dollars) as P(x) = −3x³ + 48x² − 144x, where x is paddleboards sold (in thousands). What do the zeros tell you about how many paddleboards Waterworks should produce?
dok2_anchors.answer: P(x) > 0 for 4 < x < 12, so produce between 4,000 and 12,000 paddleboards.
dok2_anchors.why_dok2: Apply a familiar procedure (factor, find zeros, sign-chart) to a real-world model with a single correct interpretation. The contextual translation is one step on top of routine execution.

dok3_anchors.source: Savvas Practice #27 (Model With Mathematics, lesson 3-5, anchors Example 4)
dok3_anchors.prompt: A rectangular storage box has length less than both its length and width (length is greatest). f(x) = x³ + 2x² − 3x represents the volume, where x is the width in feet. (a) Find the factored form of f(x). (b) Find the zeros. (c) x is the width — what do the other two factors represent? (d) Find the dimensions when V = 10 ft³.
dok3_anchors.answer: (a) x(x+3)(x−1); (b) x = 0, −3, 1; (c) (x−1) is height, (x+3) is length; (d) width = 2 ft, height = 1 ft, length = 5 ft.
dok3_anchors.why_dok3: Coordinate four sub-problems where each part depends on a strategic interpretation of the previous one — factor, then map factors to physical dimensions using the constraint 'length is greatest', then solve a non-routine cubic equation in context. No single procedure produces all four parts.

dok3_anchors.prompt: Venetta opened deli sandwich franchises in 2000. The profit P (in hundreds of dollars) of the franchises in t years is P(t) = t³ + t² − 6t. (A) Sketch a graph of the function. (B) Based on the model, during what years did Venetta not make a profit? (C) If the model is appropriate, predict Venetta's profit in 2020.
dok3_anchors.answer: (A) cubic with zeros at t = −3, 0, 2; (B) Venetta did not make a profit during the first two years (0 ≤ t < 2); (C) predicted 2020 profit ≈ $828,000 (P(20) = 8,280 hundreds of dollars).
dok3_anchors.why_dok3: Open-ended modeling task: produce a graph, interpret zeros as a real-world interval, then extrapolate well beyond the modeled range and judge model appropriateness. Requires strategic reasoning across representations, not a single procedure.
